headline: How to catch an art thief
summary: Museums and collectors face increasingly unscrupulous kleptomaniacs — is a ‘Find My Picasso’ sensor the answer?
body_text:
In 2001, wanted posters were fly-posted on walls all over Berlin. Below the large, Wild West-style “WANTED” banner in red, the image on each poster was not of a terrorist or a murderer; it wasn’t even a photograph. Instead, it showed Lucian Freud’s 1952 painting of his friend Francis Bacon. Underneath the portrait, in which Bacon’s large eyes are downcast and doleful, the text read: “For clues leading to the recovery of this small painting there is a reward of up to DM300,000. Messages will be treated with absolute confidentiality.” The painting had been stolen from the Neue Nationalgalerie on May 27, 1988. It has never been recovered.

The FBI estimates that between $4bn-$6bn of art is stolen every year. While this number might be vague (a billion here, a billion there) it suggests a staggering kleptomania. Christopher Marinello of Art Recovery International, a specialist in retrieving stolen works, tells me that the FBI’s number has been bandied around for decades. Its accuracy is de
